sections.heading: Authentication vs Authorization
sections.content:
Authentication = Who are you? (identity verification)

Authorization = What can you do? (permission checks)

In Spring Security, authentication happens first (via filters), then authorization is enforced on resources.

sections.heading: Defense in Depth
sections.content:
Never rely on a single security layer:

  • Network security (TLS, firewalls)
  • Authentication (OAuth2, JWT)
  • Authorization (roles, resource-level)
  • Input validation
  • Output encoding
  • Audit logging

sections.heading: Zero Trust
sections.content:
Assume the network is compromised. Every service-to-service call should be authenticated and authorized, not just external requests.

Use mTLS, short-lived tokens, and least-privilege service accounts.

sections.heading: Filter Chain
sections.content:
Spring Security works through a chain of servlet filters:

Request → SecurityFilterChain
  → CorsFilter
  → CsrfFilter
  → AuthenticationFilter
  → AuthorizationFilter
  → Your Controller

Each filter has a specific responsibility. Order matters.

sections.heading: OAuth2 & JWT
sections.content:
OAuth2 delegates authorization to an authorization server. The app never sees user credentials.

JWT (JSON Web Token) carries claims signed by the issuer. Validation checks signature, expiration, audience and issuer.

Header.Payload.Signature

// Payload example:
{
  "sub": "driver-123",
  "roles": ["DRIVER"],
  "exp": 1700000000
}

sections.heading: Method Security
sections.content:
@PreAuthorize("hasRole('PLANNER')")
public void assignDriver(Long deliveryId, Long driverId) {
    // Only planners can assign
}

@PostAuthorize("returnObject.driverId == principal.id")
public Delivery getDelivery(Long id) {
    // Driver can only see own deliveries
}

sections.heading: Top Risks for Microservices
sections.content:
• Broken Access Control — check resource ownership, not just roles
  • Injection — use parameterized queries always
  • Security Misconfiguration — review defaults in containers/cloud
  • Vulnerable Dependencies — scan with Dependabot/Snyk
  • SSRF — validate URLs in service-to-service calls

sections.heading: SQL Injection Prevention
sections.content:
// ❌ NEVER
String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + userId;

// ✅ ALWAYS parameterized
@Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.id = :id")
User findById(@Param("id") Long id);

sections.heading: XSS in Angular
sections.content:
Angular sanitizes by default in templates. But [innerHTML] requires care:

// Angular sanitizes this automatically
{{ userInput }}

// This is sanitized but allows safe HTML
[innerHTML]="userContent"

// ❌ DANGEROUS — bypasses sanitizer
[innerHTML]="bypassSecurityTrustHtml(userInput)"

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content:
Senior engineers think of security as layers, not as a single gate.

Key distinctions:
• Authentication (who are you?) and authorization (what can you do?) are separate concerns with separate failure modes
• OAuth2 is a delegation protocol, not an authentication protocol — OpenID Connect adds identity
• JWTs are not session tokens — they cannot be revoked individually without a revocation list
• Input validation at the API boundary prevents injection regardless of framework-level protections
• Security misconfigurations in containers, clouds and CI/CD are now more common than application-level vulnerabilities

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content:
In TRAB's security model:

• Drivers authenticate via mobile app (OAuth2 + refresh tokens with short-lived access tokens)
• Service-to-service: mTLS between internal services, API keys for external carriers
• Authorization: drivers can only see their own deliveries, warehouse managers see their warehouse only
• GDPR: driver location data retained for 90 days only, customer data anonymized after delivery completion
• External carrier API: rate-limited, IP-whitelisted, with request signing for integrity verification

sections.heading: When NOT to use
sections.content:
When NOT to add security complexity:

• Internal-only services behind a service mesh that already handles mTLS
• Development environments where security friction slows iteration (but never disable in staging)
• When adding a WAF that blocks legitimate traffic — security that prevents usage is not security
• Over-engineering session management for a service with no user state
